questions.stem: Enzymes act as biological:
questions.explanation: Enzymes are catalysts that increase the rate of reactions in cells, which benefits metabolism.

questions.stem: Metabolism is best defined as:

options.text: The complex network of interacting chemical reactions in an organism

questions.explanation: Metabolism is the complex network of interdependent and interacting chemical reactions in living organisms.

questions.stem: Why do organisms require many different enzymes?

options.text: Because enzymes are specific to particular reactions
Because one enzyme can catalyse every reaction
Because enzymes are used up in reactions

questions.explanation: Enzyme specificity means many different enzymes are needed, and control of metabolism can be exerted through them.

questions.scenario: Monomers are joined into macromolecules by condensation.
questions.stem: This type of reaction is:
questions.explanation: Anabolism builds macromolecules from monomers by condensation, such as protein synthesis and photosynthesis.

questions.scenario: Large molecules are broken down into monomers during digestion.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Catabolism breaks down macromolecules, for example by hydrolysis in digestion and oxidation in respiration.

questions.stem: Enzymes are which type of protein?
questions.explanation: Enzymes are globular proteins with an active site for catalysis.

questions.stem: The active site of an enzyme is:

options.text: A region of a few amino acids where catalysis occurs
The whole surface of the enzyme

questions.explanation: The active site is made of a few amino acids, but the whole 3D structure ensures it has the right properties for catalysis.

questions.scenario: A substrate binds to an enzyme's active site.
questions.stem: According to the induced-fit model, what happens?

options.text: Both the enzyme and substrate change shape on binding

questions.explanation: In induced-fit binding, both substrate and enzyme change shape when binding occurs.

questions.stem: What allows a substrate and active site to come together?
questions.explanation: Molecular motion causes substrate-active site collisions; sometimes substrates or enzymes are immobilized.

questions.scenario: An enzyme only catalyses one particular reaction.
questions.stem: This specificity is due to the:

options.text: Shape of its active site matching a specific substrate

questions.explanation: The structure of the active site determines enzyme-substrate specificity; disrupting it causes denaturation.

questions.scenario: An enzyme's active site loses its shape at high temperature.
questions.stem: The enzyme has been:
questions.explanation: When the active site's shape is disrupted, the enzyme is denatured and can no longer bind its substrate.

questions.scenario: The temperature is raised well above an enzyme's optimum.
questions.stem: What happens to the reaction rate?

options.text: It falls as the enzyme denatures

questions.explanation: Above the optimum, denaturation reduces enzyme activity, so the reaction rate falls.

questions.scenario: Substrate concentration is increased at constant enzyme concentration.
questions.stem: The rate of reaction will:

options.text: Increase then level off as enzymes become saturated

questions.explanation: Rate rises with substrate concentration until all active sites are occupied, then it plateaus.

questions.scenario: A generalized sketch graph shows how rate changes with pH.
questions.stem: According to NOS, such a sketch graph is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Generalized sketch graphs of relationships are models, which can be evaluated using experimental results.

questions.scenario: The effect of temperature on enzyme rate is explained using collision theory.
questions.stem: Higher temperature (below the optimum) increases rate because:

options.text: Molecules move faster, so collisions are more frequent

questions.explanation: Below the optimum, higher temperature increases molecular motion and collision frequency, raising the rate.

questions.stem: How do enzymes affect the activation energy of a reaction?

options.text: They remove the need for products
They have no effect on it

questions.explanation: Enzymes lower the activation energy needed for a reaction, speeding it up.

questions.stem: In an enzyme-catalysed reaction, energy is required to:
questions.explanation: Energy is needed to break substrate bonds, while forming product bonds releases energy.

questions.stem: The benefit to a cell of enzymes speeding up reactions is that:

options.text: Reactions occur fast enough to sustain life at body temperature

questions.explanation: By increasing reaction rates, enzymes allow metabolism to proceed fast enough to sustain life.

questions.stem: Which is an example of an anabolic reaction?

options.text: Digestion of protein into amino acids

questions.explanation: Anabolism builds larger molecules; glycogen formation, protein synthesis and photosynthesis are examples.

questions.stem: The induced-fit model improves on the older 'lock and key' idea by recognising that:

options.text: The active site changes shape to fit the substrate
The active site is completely rigid

questions.explanation: Induced fit recognises that both enzyme and substrate change shape on binding, unlike a rigid lock and key.

questions.scenario: An enzyme works best at pH 7 and poorly at pH 3.
questions.stem: The low activity at pH 3 is because:

options.text: The extreme pH disrupts the active site (denaturation)

questions.explanation: Extreme pH away from the optimum can denature the enzyme, disrupting the active site and lowering activity.

questions.scenario: A student times how long an enzyme takes to break down a substrate.
questions.stem: From this they can determine the:

options.text: Amino acid sequence of the enzyme

questions.explanation: Measurements allow determination of reaction rates, from experimental or secondary data.

questions.stem: Control over metabolism can be exerted mainly through:
questions.explanation: Because enzymes are specific, controlling them provides control over the metabolic network.

questions.stem: Even though the active site is only a few amino acids, its correct shape depends on:

options.text: Interactions across the whole 3D structure of the enzyme
The pH of the blood only

questions.explanation: Interactions between amino acids throughout the enzyme give the active site its catalytic properties.

questions.scenario: An enzyme is embedded in a membrane.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Enzymes can be immobilized by being embedded in membranes; sometimes substrates are immobilized instead.

questions.stem: Overall, an enzyme-catalysed reaction that releases energy does so because:

options.text: More energy is released forming product bonds than is used breaking substrate bonds
Enzymes add energy to the products

questions.explanation: Breaking substrate bonds needs energy; forming product bonds yields energy, giving the overall energy change.

questions.scenario: A graph of rate against substrate concentration plateaus at high concentration.
questions.stem: The plateau occurs because:

options.text: All active sites are occupied (enzymes saturated)

questions.explanation: At high substrate concentration the enzymes are saturated, so adding more substrate does not increase the rate.

questions.stem: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a reaction and is:

options.text: Not used up in the reaction

questions.explanation: Catalysts, including enzymes, speed up reactions without being used up.

questions.stem: Which pairing of process and type is correct?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis builds carbon compounds (anabolic); respiration oxidises substrates (catabolic).

questions.stem: Enzyme-substrate specificity means that an enzyme:

options.text: Catalyses only reactions of substrates that fit its active site
Has many identical active sites for all substrates

questions.explanation: Specificity arises because only substrates matching the active site's shape can bind and react.

questions.scenario: Chemical digestion of food occurs in the gut lumen.
questions.stem: This is an example of an:
questions.explanation: Digestion in the gut is extracellular; glycolysis and the Krebs cycle are intracellular examples.

questions.scenario: Glycolysis and the Krebs cycle occur inside cells.
questions.stem: These are examples of:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis and the Krebs cycle are intracellular enzyme-catalysed reactions.

questions.stem: Heat is generated by metabolic reactions because:
questions.explanation: Because metabolic energy transfers are not 100% efficient, heat is inevitably generated.

questions.stem: Mammals and birds depend on metabolic heat production for:
questions.explanation: Endotherms such as mammals and birds use the heat from inefficient metabolism to keep body temperature constant.

questions.stem: Which metabolic pathway is cyclical?
questions.explanation: The Krebs cycle and Calvin cycle are cyclical; glycolysis is a linear pathway.

questions.stem: Glycolysis is an example of a metabolic pathway that is:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis is a linear pathway, whereas the Krebs and Calvin cycles are cyclical.

questions.scenario: An inhibitor binds to a site away from the active site, changing the enzyme's shape.
questions.stem: This is:

options.text: Non-competitive inhibition at an allosteric site
Competitive inhibition at the active site

questions.explanation: Binding at an allosteric site causes conformational change altering the active site — non-competitive inhibition.

questions.stem: Binding of a non-competitive inhibitor to an allosteric site is:
questions.explanation: Binding at an allosteric site is reversible, unlike mechanism-based (irreversible) inhibition.

questions.scenario: An inhibitor binds reversibly to the active site, competing with the substrate.
questions.stem: This is:
questions.explanation: Competitive inhibitors bind reversibly to the active site, competing with the substrate; statins are an example.

questions.scenario: Substrate concentration is increased in the presence of a competitive inhibitor.
questions.stem: What is the effect on the inhibition?

options.text: It can be overcome, as substrate outcompetes the inhibitor

questions.explanation: Adding substrate reduces competitive inhibition, since substrate and inhibitor compete for the active site.

questions.stem: Statins are used as an example of:
questions.explanation: Statins are the syllabus example of competitive inhibitors.

questions.scenario: The end product of a pathway inhibits an enzyme earlier in that pathway.
questions.stem: This regulation is called:
questions.explanation: In feedback inhibition an end product inhibits an earlier enzyme; the isoleucine pathway is the example.

questions.stem: The benefit of feedback inhibition is that it:

options.text: Prevents wasteful overproduction of the end product

questions.explanation: Feedback inhibition stops a pathway when enough product is present, avoiding waste and regulating metabolism.

questions.scenario: Penicillin binds irreversibly to an enzyme, chemically changing its active site.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Mechanism-based inhibition involves irreversible binding that chemically changes the active site; penicillin is the example.

questions.scenario: Some bacteria have altered transpeptidases.
questions.stem: This change confers:

options.text: The inability to make cell walls

questions.explanation: Changes to transpeptidases confer resistance to penicillin, which normally inhibits these enzymes.

questions.stem: Which statement about an allosteric site is correct?

options.text: Only specific substances can bind to it
Any molecule can bind to it
It is the same as the active site

questions.explanation: Allosteric sites bind only specific substances, whose binding causes conformational changes affecting catalysis.

questions.scenario: The effect of a non-competitive inhibitor is compared with a competitive one.
questions.stem: Increasing substrate concentration will:

options.text: Reverse competitive inhibition but not non-competitive inhibition

questions.explanation: Extra substrate overcomes competitive inhibition (same site) but not non-competitive inhibition (allosteric site).

questions.stem: Heat production by metabolism is best described as:

options.text: An inevitable consequence of inefficient energy transfer
A sign that enzymes have denatured

questions.explanation: Heat generation is inevitable because metabolic reactions are not 100% efficient; endotherms exploit it.

questions.stem: In the isoleucine pathway, the end product isoleucine acts as:

options.text: An inhibitor of an earlier enzyme
A substrate for the first enzyme

questions.explanation: In feedback inhibition, isoleucine inhibits an earlier enzyme in its own synthesis pathway.

questions.stem: How does mechanism-based inhibition differ from competitive inhibition?

options.text: It binds irreversibly and chemically changes the active site
It binds reversibly and can be outcompeted
It only works at high temperature

questions.explanation: Mechanism-based inhibition is irreversible, chemically altering the active site, unlike reversible competitive inhibition.
